10.1016/j.jmp.2022.102708
Doignon and Falmagne (1985)  defined a knowledge structure as a pair  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            K 
                            ) 
                         
                       in which  
                         Q 
                       is a non-empty set and  
                         K 
                       is a family of subsets of  
                         Q 
                      , containing at least  
                         Q 
                       and the empty set  
                         0̸ 
                      . Here, the empty set  
                         0̸ 
                       refers to the naive knowledge state, and  
                         Q 
                       refers to the state of complete mastery. The set  
                         Q 
                       is called the domain of the knowledge structure. Its elements are referred to as items and the subsets in the family  
                         K 
                       are labelled (knowledge) states. A knowledge state represents the subset of items in the considered domain that an individual masters. More specific structural assumptions on the collection of knowledge states have received particular attention, among them the knowledge spaces (closed under union), and the quasi-ordinal knowledge spaces (closed under union and intersection).

Knowledge structure theory (abbr. KST:  Doignon & Falmagne, 1985, 1999; Falmagne & Doignon, 2011 ) conceives items as being dichotomous. It categorizes the responses into the dichotomy of “mastering” vs. “not mastering” an item (or, “solving” vs. “not solving”, respectively). Recently,  Heller (2021)  and  Stefanutti, Anselmi, de Chiusole, and Spoto (2020)  extended the binary case to a set of response values that forms a (complete) lattice, which can be identified with different types, or degrees of mastering. Moreover, they provided some characterizations of the resulting polytomous knowledge structures that are based on the domain  
                         Q 
                       consisting of items and the lattice consisting of response values. Such work opens the field to a systematic generalization of many KST concepts and results to the polytomous case.

Recall that a binary relation  
                         R 
                       on a set  
                         B 
                       is a quasi order if  
                         R 
                       is reflexive and transitive; a quasi order  
                         R 
                       on  
                         B 
                       is a partial order if  
                         R 
                       is antisymmetric. Given the set  
                         L 
                       of response values and a partial order  
                         ≤ 
                       on  
                         L 
                      , the relational structure  
                         
                            ( 
                            L 
                            , 
                            ≤ 
                            ) 
                         
                       is called a response scale. The basic assumption in what follows is that  
                         
                            ( 
                            L 
                            , 
                            ≤ 
                            ) 
                         
                       is a lattice. This means that for all pairs of elements in  
                         L 
                       there exist a supremum (least upper bound w.r.t.  
                         ≤ 
                      ) and an infimum (greatest lower bound w.r.t.  
                         ≤ 
                      ). Using an algebraic notation, suprema and infima are denoted by the binary operations join  
                         ∨ 
                       and meet  
                         ∧ 
                      , i.e.,  
                         
                            v 
                            ∨ 
                            w 
                            = 
                            sup 
                            
                               { 
                               v 
                               , 
                               w 
                               } 
                            
                         
                       and  
                         
                            v 
                            ∧ 
                            w 
                            = 
                            inf 
                            
                               { 
                               v 
                               , 
                               w 
                               } 
                            
                         
                       for all  
                         
                            v 
                            , 
                            w 
                            ∈ 
                            L 
                         
                      . Further,  
                         
                            ( 
                            L 
                            , 
                            ≤ 
                            ) 
                         
                       is a complete lattice if for every subset  
                         
                            S 
                            ⊆ 
                            L 
                         
                      ,  
                         S 
                       has both a supremum (least upper bound w.r.t.  
                         ≤ 
                      ) and an infimum (greatest lower bound w.r.t.  
                         ≤ 
                      ), where supremum of  
                         S 
                       and infimum of  
                         S 
                       are denoted by  
                         
                            sup 
                            S 
                         
                       and  
                         
                            inf 
                            S 
                         
                       respectively. For a complete lattice  
                         
                            ( 
                            L 
                            , 
                            ≤ 
                            ) 
                         
                      ,  
                         
                            inf 
                            L 
                         
                       and  
                         
                            sup 
                            L 
                         
                       always are denoted by  
                         0 
                       and  
                         1 
                       in this paper, respectively. A single response value  
                         
                            x 
                            ∈ 
                            L 
                         
                       is said to be irreducible (see  Stefanutti et al., 2020 ) if, for all subsets  
                         
                            G 
                            ⊆ 
                            L 
                         
                      ,  
                         
                            sup 
                            G 
                            = 
                            x 
                         
                       implies  
                         
                            x 
                            ∈ 
                            G 
                         
                      . For a complete lattice  
                         
                            ( 
                            L 
                            , 
                            ≤ 
                            ) 
                         
                      ,  
                         
                            
                               L 
                            
                            
                               + 
                            
                         
                       always denotes the subset of all the irreducible response values of  
                         L 
                      . By convention, the supremum of an empty set of response values is  
                         0 
                      
 ( 
                         
                            sup 
                            0̸ 
                            = 
                            0 
                         
                      ), therefore  
                         
                            0 
                            ⁄ 
                            ∈ 
                            
                               
                                  L 
                               
                               
                                  + 
                               
                            
                         
                       (see  Stefanutti et al., 2020 ).

Let  
                         Q 
                       be a set of items and  
                         
                            ( 
                            L 
                            , 
                            ≤ 
                            ) 
                         
                       a complete lattice. Then each polytomous state is formally a mapping  
                         
                            K 
                            : 
                            Q 
                            → 
                            L 
                         
                      . Let  
                         
                            
                               L 
                            
                            
                               Q 
                            
                         
                       denote the collection of all possible polytomous states, i.e., the set of all mappings  
                         
                            Q 
                            → 
                            L 
                         
                      . Alternatively, a polytomous state  
                         K 
                       may be considered as a particular subset of  
                         
                            Q 
                            × 
                            L 
                         
                      . By convention, two trivial polytomous states are described as follows.

(1)  
                         
                            Φ 
                            : 
                            Q 
                            → 
                            L 
                         
                       is defined by  
                         
                            Φ 
                            
                               ( 
                               q 
                               ) 
                            
                            = 
                            0 
                         
                       for all  
                         
                            q 
                            ∈ 
                            Q 
                         
                      .

(2)  
                         
                            Ω 
                            : 
                            Q 
                            → 
                            L 
                         
                       is defined by  
                         
                            Ω 
                            
                               ( 
                               q 
                               ) 
                            
                            = 
                            1 
                         
                       for all  
                         
                            q 
                            ∈ 
                            Q 
                         
                      .

The definition of a state as a mapping from items to response values induces a natural ordering on the collection  
                         
                            
                               L 
                            
                            
                               Q 
                            
                         
                       (see  Stefanutti et al., 2020 ; sometimes termed pointwise order, see e.g.  Davey & Priestley, 2002 ): given two mappings  
                         
                            
                               
                                  K 
                               
                               
                                  1 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  K 
                               
                               
                                  2 
                               
                            
                            ∈ 
                            
                               
                                  L 
                               
                               
                                  Q 
                               
                            
                         
                      ,  
                         
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        K 
                                     
                                     
                                        1 
                                     
                                  
                                  ⊑ 
                                  
                                     
                                        K 
                                     
                                     
                                        2 
                                     
                                  
                                  ⟺ 
                                  
                                     
                                        K 
                                     
                                     
                                        1 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     q 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  ≤ 
                                  
                                     
                                        K 
                                     
                                     
                                        2 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     q 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  
                                  f 
                                  o 
                                  r 
                                  
                                  a 
                                  l 
                                  l 
                                  
                                  q 
                                  ∈ 
                                  Q 
                                  .

Clearly,  
                         
                            Φ 
                            ⊑ 
                            K 
                            ⊑ 
                            Ω 
                         
                       for all  
                         
                            K 
                            ∈ 
                            
                               
                                  L 
                               
                               
                                  Q 
                               
                            
                         
                      . In addition, it is not difficult to see that  
                         ⊑ 
                       is indeed a partial order on the set  
                         
                            
                               L 
                            
                            
                               Q 
                            
                         
                       of mappings. Indeed,  
                         
                            
                               L 
                            
                            
                               Q 
                            
                         
                       is itself a complete lattice, since infima and suprema exist for all of its subsets in virtue of the existence of infima and suprema for all subsets of  
                         L 
                       (see,  Stefanutti et al., 2020 ). Now we may give the following definition that is an adaptation to the polytomous framework of Definition 2.1.2 in  Falmagne and Doignon (2011)  and a slight change for polytomous knowledge structures in the sense of both  Heller (2021)  and  Stefanutti et al. (2020) .

Definition 1 
                         Let  
                               Q 
                             be a set of items and  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  L 
                                  , 
                                  ≤ 
                                  ) 
                               
                             a complete lattice of response values. A polytomous knowledge structure on the pair  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  L 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is a triple  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  L 
                                  , 
                                  K 
                                  ) 
                               
                             where  
                               K 
                             is a collection of polytomous states containing at least  
                               Φ 
                             and  
                               Ω 
                            . Occasionally, we shall say that  
                               K 
                             is a polytomous knowledge structure to mean that  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  L 
                                  , 
                                  K 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is a polytomous knowledge structure.

Let  
                               Q 
                             be a set of items and  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  L 
                                  , 
                                  ≤ 
                                  ) 
                               
                             a complete lattice of response values. A polytomous knowledge structure on the pair  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  L 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is a triple  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  L 
                                  , 
                                  K 
                                  ) 
                               
                             where  
                               K 
                             is a collection of polytomous states containing at least  
                               Φ 
                             and  
                               Ω 
                            . Occasionally, we shall say that  
                               K 
                             is a polytomous knowledge structure to mean that  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  L 
                                  , 
                                  K 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is a polytomous knowledge structure.

The polytomous knowledge structure  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            L 
                            , 
                            K 
                            ) 
                         
                       is finite if  
                         K 
                       is finite (see,  Stefanutti et al., 2020 ). It is clear that, depending on the properties of the two sets  
                         Q 
                       and  
                         L 
                      , the  
                         K 
                       may be either finite or infinite. For example, if both  
                         Q 
                       and L are finite then  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            L 
                            , 
                            K 
                            ) 
                         
                       is obviously finite (see,  Stefanutti et al., 2020 ).

Remark 1 
                         The conception of  Definition 1  can reduce to the classical notion of a knowledge structure when restricted to the dichotomous case, which is stronger than that of  Schrepp (1997)  and  Stefanutti et al. (2020) , who consider any non-empty subset of  
                               
                                  
                                     L 
                                  
                                  
                                     Q 
                                  
                               
                             a polytomous knowledge structure; and weaker than that of  Heller (2021) , who considers a polytomous knowledge structure  
                               K 
                             containing at least  
                               Φ 
                             and  
                               Ω 
                            , and  
                               
                                  ⋃ 
                                  K 
                                  = 
                                  Q 
                                  × 
                                  L 
                               
                            .

The conception of  Definition 1  can reduce to the classical notion of a knowledge structure when restricted to the dichotomous case, which is stronger than that of  Schrepp (1997)  and  Stefanutti et al. (2020) , who consider any non-empty subset of  
                               
                                  
                                     L 
                                  
                                  
                                     Q 
                                  
                               
                             a polytomous knowledge structure; and weaker than that of  Heller (2021) , who considers a polytomous knowledge structure  
                               K 
                             containing at least  
                               Φ 
                             and  
                               Ω 
                            , and  
                               
                                  ⋃ 
                                  K 
                                  = 
                                  Q 
                                  × 
                                  L 
                               
                            .

The following example shows an adaptation of a dichotomous knowledge structure in the polytomous framework.

Definition 2 
                         
                            Stefanutti et al., 2020 
                         
                         Given a set  
                               Q 
                             of items and a complete lattice  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  L 
                                  , 
                                  ≤ 
                                  ) 
                               
                             of response values, let  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  L 
                                  , 
                                  K 
                                  ) 
                               
                             be a polytomous knowledge structure. For a subfamily  
                               
                                  G 
                                  ⊆ 
                                  K 
                               
                            , define a mapping  
                               
                                  ⊔ 
                                  G 
                                  : 
                                  Q 
                                  → 
                                  L 
                               
                             by  
                               
                                  ⊔ 
                                  G 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     q 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  = 
                                  sup 
                                  
                                     { 
                                     K 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        q 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     : 
                                     K 
                                     ∈ 
                                     G 
                                     } 
                                  
                               
                             for all  
                               
                                  q 
                                  ∈ 
                                  Q 
                               
                            . Note that the mapping  
                               
                                  ⊔ 
                                  G 
                               
                             is well defined because the lattice  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  L 
                                  , 
                                  ≤ 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is complete. 
                         (1)  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  L 
                                  , 
                                  K 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is called closed under the supremum if  
                               
                                  ⊔ 
                                  G 
                                  ∈ 
                                  K 
                               
                             for all  
                               
                                  G 
                                  ⊆ 
                                  K 
                               
                            . 
                         (2)  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  L 
                                  , 
                                  K 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is called a polytomous knowledge space if it is closed under the supremum.

The following polytomous attribution is an adaptation to the polytomous framework of Definition 5.1.2 in  Falmagne and Doignon (2011) , which was introduced by  Stefanutti et al. (2020) .

Definition 3 
                         
                            Stefanutti et al., 2020 
                         
                         Given a set  
                               Q 
                             of items and a complete lattice  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  L 
                                  , 
                                  ≤ 
                                  )

                                  Q

                                     q

                                  0̸

                               μ

                                           q

                                  0

                             Then  
                               μ 
                             is a polytomous surmise function, which is an adaptation of  
                               σ 
                             in the polytomous framework.

In KST, there exist many interesting and important relations between the knowledge structures and the attributions (see e.g.  Doignon & Falmagne, 1985; Falmagne & Doignon, 2011; Ge & Li, 2020; Ge & Lin, 2021; Shen, Lin, & Ge, 2021 ). Likewise, the polytomous knowledge structures are closely related to the polytomous attributions. Specially, Galois connections between polytomous knowledge structures and polytomous attributions can show some relations between them (see e.g.  Heller, 2021 ).

An early concept of a Galois connection is closely related to that of a “residuated mapping” (see e.g.  Blyth & Janowitz, 1972 ). The following definition for Galois connections slightly extends the early concept of a Galois connection, by allowing quasi ordered sets instead of ordered sets. This construction comes from  Doignon and Falmagne (1985) . More background on Galois connections can be found in  Birkhoff (1967)  or  Barbut and Monjardet (1970) , for instance.

                            Doignon & Falmagne, 1985 
                         
                         Let

                            .

As a celebrated result,  Birkhoff (1937)  confirmed the existence of a one-to-one correspondence between the collection of all quasi ordinal spaces on the domain  
                         Q 
                       and the collection of all quasi orders on  
                         Q 
                      . The idea of deriving Birkhoff’s Theorem from a Galois connection is due to  Monjardet (1970)  (cf. Corollary 8.4.3 of  Falmagne & Doignon, 2011 ). Based on the Birkhoff result,  Doignon and Falmagne (1985)  established a Galois connection between the collection of all knowledge structures on a finite domain  
                         Q 
                       and the collection of all attributions on  
                         Q 
                      , where the closed elements in the two collections are the knowledge spaces and the surmise functions respectively. In order to extend the finite domain to the arbitrary domain in the aforementioned result,  Falmagne and Doignon (2011)  introduced granularity in knowledge structures and attributions to establish a Galois connection between the collection of all granular knowledge structures on the domain  
                         Q 
                       and the collection of all granular attributions on  
                         Q 
                      , where the closed elements in the two collections are the granular knowledge spaces and the surmise functions respectively (see Theorem 8.5.3 of  Falmagne & Doignon, 2011 ). As the more general case (without requiring granularity),  Shen et al. (2021)  used the derivations between knowledge structures and attributions to establish a Galois connection between the collection of all knowledge structures on the domain  
                         Q 
                       and the collection of all attributions on  
                         Q 
                      , where the closed elements in the two collections are the knowledge spaces and the complete attributions respectively. In addition, there are some Galois connections between knowledge structures and relations. For example,  Falmagne and Doignon (2011)  established a Galois connection between the collection of all knowledge structures on the domain  
                         Q 
                       and the collection of all binary relations on  
                         Q 
                      , where the closed elements in the two collections are the quasi ordinal spaces and the quasi orders respectively (see Theorem 8.4.2 of  Falmagne & Doignon, 2011 );  Koppen and Doignon (1990)  established a Galois connection between the collection of all knowledge structures on the domain  
                         Q 
                       and the collection of all association relations for  
                         Q 
                      , where the closed elements in the two collections are the knowledge spaces and the entailment relations respectively (see Theorem 8.6.2 of  Falmagne & Doignon, 2011 ).

It should be noticed that, in various aforementioned Galois connections, a number of one-to-one correspondences were established in ordinary (dichotomous) KST. Recently,  Stefanutti et al. (2020)  generalized the Birkhoff one-to-one correspondence to polytomous items. For a non-empty set  
                         Q 
                       and a complete lattice  
                         
                            ( 
                            L 
                            , 
                            ≤ 
                            ) 
                         
                      , they established a one-to-one correspondence between the collection of all the granular polytomous knowledge spaces on  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            L 
                            ) 
                         
                       and the collection of all the polytomous surmise functions on  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            L 
                            ) 
                         
                      . In addition, following  Doignon and Falmagne (1985) ,  Heller (2021)  established a one-to-one correspondence between specific relations on  
                         
                            Q 
                            × 
                            L 
                         
                       and particular collections of polytomous knowledge states by setting up a Galois connection between the collection of all binary relations on  
                         
                            Q 
                            × 
                            L 
                         
                       and the collection of all families consisting of the polytomous knowledge states on  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            L 
                            ) 
                         
                      , where  
                         
                            Q 
                            , 
                            L 
                         
                       are finite non-empty sets, and  
                         
                            ( 
                            L 
                            , 
                            ≤ 
                            ) 
                         
                       is a lattice. Without doubt, these results are important contributions that open a “beautiful picture” for the development of polytomous KST. Notice that the granularity of polytomous knowledge structures (polytomous attributions) was appended in the results of  Stefanutti et al. (2020)  and the finiteness of two sets  
                         Q 
                      ,  
                         L 
                       was assumed in the discussion of  Heller (2021) . In addition, the one-to-one correspondence given by  Stefanutti et al. (2020)  does not deal with Galois connections and the Galois connection established by  Heller (2021)  does not deal with polytomous attributions. All these lead us to explore more general results for Galois connections between polytomous knowledge structures and polytomous attributions. That is, we are interested in the following question.

Question 
                         Let  
                               Q 
                             be a set of items and  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  L 
                                  , 
                                  ≤ 
                                  ) 
                               
                             be a complete lattice of response values. 
                         (1) How to establish a Galois connection between the collection of all polytomous knowledge structures on  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  L 
                                  ) 
                               
                             and the collection of all polytomous attributions on  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  L 
                                  ) 
                               
                            ? 
                         (2) How to establish a Galois connection between the collection of all granular polytomous knowledge structures on  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  L 
                                  ) 
                               
                             and the collection of some particular polytomous attributions on  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  L 
                                  ) 
                               
                            ? 
                         (3) How to characterize these particular polytomous attributions presented in (2)?

Let  
                               Q 
                             be a set of items and  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  L 
                                  , 
                                  ≤ 
                                  ) 
                               
                             be a complete lattice of response values.

(1) How to establish a Galois connection between the collection of all polytomous knowledge structures on  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  L 
                                  ) 
                               
                             and the collection of all polytomous attributions on  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  L 
                                  ) 
                               
                            ?

(2) How to establish a Galois connection between the collection of all granular polytomous knowledge structures on  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  L 
                                  ) 
                               
                             and the collection of some particular polytomous attributions on  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  L 
                                  ) 
                               
                            ?

(3) How to characterize these particular polytomous attributions presented in (2)?

Our discussion spreads around the above Question. The main aim of this paper is to give some affirmative answers on the above Question and to provide some new theoretical results for the generalization of Galois connections in polytomous KST.

Throughout this paper, by default if not mentioned otherwise, the letter  
                         Q 
                       always denotes a nonempty set of items and  
                         
                            ( 
                            L 
                            , 
                            ≤ 
                            ) 
                         
                       a complete lattice consisting of a nonempty set  
                         L 
                       of response values with a partial order  
                         ≤ 
                       on  
                         L 
                      , where  
                         Q 
                       and  
                         L 
                       need not be finite. All polytomous knowledge structures (resp. all polytomous attributions) are on the pair  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            L 
                            ) 
                         
                      ; all polytomous knowledge structures (resp. polytomous knowledge spaces) are abbreviated to polytomous structures (resp. polytomous spaces).

The following two propositions, respectively, give two derivations that show some relations between polytomous structures and polytomous attributions.

Proposition 1 
                         
                            Let  
                            
                               μ 
                             
                            be a polytomous attribution. Put  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        K 
                                     
                                     
                                        μ 
                                     
                                  
                                  = 
                                  
                                     
                                        K 
                                     
                                     
                                        μ 
                                     
                                     
                                        ∘ 
                                     
                                  
                                  ⋃ 
                                  
                                     { 
                                     Φ 
                                     , 
                                     Ω 
                                     } 
                                  
                               
                            
                            . Here,  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              K 
                                           
                                           
                                              μ 
                                           
                                           
                                              ∘ 
                                           
                                        
                                        = 
                                        
                                           { 
                                           K 
                                           ∈ 
                                           
                                              
                                                 L 
                                              
                                              
                                                 Q 
                                              
                                           
                                           : 
                                           f 
                                           o 
                                           r 
                                           
                                           e 
                                           a 
                                           c 
                                           h 
                                           
                                           q 
                                           ∈ 
                                           Q 
                                           , 
                                           
                                           t 
                                           h 
                                           e 
                                           r 
                                           e 
                                           
                                           i 
                                           s 
                                           
                                           C 
                                           ∈ 
                                           μ 
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              q 
                                              ) 
                                           
                                           
                                           s 
                                           u 
                                           c 
                                           h 
                                           
                                           t 
                                           h 
                                           a 
                                           t 
                                           
                                           
                                           C 
                                           ⊑ 
                                           K 
                                           
                                           a 
                                           n 
                                           d 
                                           
                                           C 
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              q 
                                              ) 
                                           
                                           = 
                                           K 
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              q 
                                              )

In order to give a comparison method of dichotomous attributions,  Falmagne and Doignon (2011)  introduced a quasi order on the collection of all dichotomous attributions (see Definition 5.5.1 in  Falmagne & Doignon, 2011 ). By means of the quasi order,  Shen et al. (2021)  established a Galois connection between dichotomous knowledge structures and dichotomous attributions. Inspired by this, we introduce a quasi order on the collection of all polytomous attributions in the next notation, which is an adaptation to the polytomous framework of Definition 5.5.1 in  Falmagne and Doignon (2011)  and will be used to establish a Galois connection between polytomous structures and polytomous attributions.

Notation 2 
                         
                            
                               F 
                             denotes the collection of all polytomous attributions on the pair  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  L 
                                  ) 
                               
                            .

                                  F

The following definition is an adaptation to the polytomous framework of Definition 6 in  Shen et al. (2021) , which describes the completeness of polytomous attributions.

                                        ′ 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     q 
                                     ) 
                                  
                               
                            . It follows that  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        C 
                                     
                                     
                                        ′ 
                                     
                                  
                                  ∈ 
                                  μ 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     q 
                                     ) 
                                  
                               
                             because  
                               μ 
                             satisfies  Definition 5 (2). □

In KST, resolubility of knowledge structures is defined by resolubility of the derived attributions (see Definition 5.6.5 of  Falmagne & Doignon, 2011 ). Likewise, we define the completeness of polytomous structures by the completeness of the derived polytomous attributions.

Definition 6 
                         A polytomous structure  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  L 
                                  , 
                                  K 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is said complete if  
                               
                                  K 
                                  = 
                                  
                                     
                                        K 
                                     
                                     
                                        μ 
                                     
                                  
                               
                             for some complete polytomous attribution  
                               μ 
                            .

A polytomous structure  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  L 
                                  , 
                                  K 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is said complete if  
                               
                                  K 
                                  = 
                                  
                                     
                                        K 
                                     
                                     
                                        μ 
                                     
                                  
                               
                             for some complete polytomous attribution  
                               μ 
                            .

Remark 2 
                         We have an explicit characterization of the complete polytomous attributions (see  Definition 5 ). However, we do not have a direct characterization of the complete polytomous structures. In Sections  3 and 4 , we shall show that every polytomous space is a complete polytomous structure (see  Proposition 4  and  Lemma 5 ). Furthermore, it shall be shown that a polytomous structure is completes if and only if it is a polytomous space under  Assumption 1  (see  Proposition 4  and  Corollary 4 ) where  Assumption 1  cannot be omitted (see  Proposition 4  and  Example 6 ).

We have an explicit characterization of the complete polytomous attributions (see  Definition 5 ). However, we do not have a direct characterization of the complete polytomous structures. In Sections  3 and 4 , we shall show that every polytomous space is a complete polytomous structure (see  Proposition 4  and  Lemma 5 ). Furthermore, it shall be shown that a polytomous structure is completes if and only if it is a polytomous space under  Assumption 1  (see  Proposition 4  and  Corollary 4 ) where  Assumption 1  cannot be omitted (see  Proposition 4  and  Example 6 ).

In this section, we establish a Galois connection that is based on the following lemmas and Propositions.
